The model favors Spain at 91%, leaving Cape Verde with a negligible 0.0% win probability. The underdog's only realistic path involves a draw, which the model prices at 9%. Spain enters as a heavy favorite in this neutral-venue fixture.

The spread reflects a highly asymmetric contest. Spain's dominance in the win column leaves little room for competitive narrative; a draw remains the sole plausible alternative outcome. The match is unlikely to be close in expectation, though the 9% draw probability suggests occasional scenarios where neither side breaks through.

The model and market align closely on the Spain win likelihood—the model at 91%, the sportsbook consensus at 88%, a gap of 3 percentage points. The market assigns Cape Verde a 4% win chance, whereas the model rounds to zero. Both sources agree a draw is unlikely but possible. The discrepancy in Cape Verde's tail probability reflects minor disagreement on the distribution of upsets, not a material divergence in the headline forecast.
